content: The Mound Next Door
A story by:
credit:
Kayla Payne, Ashim F. Talukdar, Victor Morgan.
1 June. 2026

content: A story about a hill that's not a hill, the Indigenous people who lived here long before us, and one of Ohio's greatest archaeological treasures hiding in plain sight.
Ohio University

Between Athens and The Plains stands a 40-foot mound made of earth that has survived centuries of change. It has endured farming, road construction, and the passage of time. Known as the Hartman Mound, it is the best-preserved remaining mound of the Wolfe Plains Group, but it is far from the only one.

The Wolfe Plains Group was once a vast network of burial mounds, ceremonial earthworks, and sacred spaces built by the Adena people beginning around 800 BCE. Together, these sites formed one of the most significant prehistoric landscapes in southeastern Ohio and offer insight into the lives, beliefs, and traditions of the people who built them.

<iframe title="Wolfe Plains Mound Group" aria-label="Locator map" id="datawrapper-chart-EcfQK" src="https://datawrapper.dwcdn.net/EcfQK/2/" scrolling="no" frameborder="0" style="width: 0; min-width: 100% !important; border: none;" height="577" data-external="1"></iframe><script type="text/javascript">window.addEventListener("message",function(a){if(void 0!==a.data["datawrapper-height"]){var e=document.querySelectorAll("iframe");for(var t in a.data["datawrapper-height"])for(var r,i=0;r=e[i];i++)if(r.contentWindow===a.source){var d=a.data["datawrapper-height"][t]+"px";r.style.height=d}}});</script>

That's roughly 2,800 years ago. While the Roman Republic was still taking shape across the Atlantic, the Adena people were leaving their mark on what is now Ohio. They constructed impressive mounds and earthworks, maintained long-distance trade networks that reached the Gulf of Mexico, and developed traditions that continue to capture the attention of archaeologists today.
Time Warp
WARNING: You are about to enter the Time Machine...

Please always keep your hands and feet inside the scroll bar. We are not responsible for existential crises caused by realizing a suburb used to be a ceremonial sacred site. Also, don't leave in the middle. You might get stuck in the time loop. We speak from experience.
Ready? Let's go back.
ERA 1 — 800 BCE to 100 CE
Before Ohio Had a Name
'Why were the mounds built?'

The Adena people did not arrive in what is now Athens County by accident. They selected this landscape with purpose, transforming the Wolfe Plains into a carefully designed ceremonial center. Here, burial mounds, sacred enclosures, and gathering spaces formed a place where communities came together to honor their ancestors, conduct rituals, and mark important moments in life and death.

Among these earthworks stood the Hartman Mound, one of the largest mounds in the Wolfe Plains group. Its construction was neither quick nor simple. Generation after generation, the mound grew through a deliberate process: ceremonial offerings were placed, wooden structures were erected for burials, and layers of earth were carefully added above them. Each layer represented another chapter in a tradition that endured for centuries.

Today, the mound remains as evidence of a society whose vision, engineering, and spiritual traditions shaped the landscape long before written history reached Ohio.

Hartman Mound, now the most visible survivor of the group, was one of the largest. Built in layers, like a very slow, very sacred lasagna: ceremonial deposits, wooden burial structures, then earth piled on top. Rinse and repeat over generations.
ERA 2 — Early 1800s
New Neighbors Arrive (And They're Bringing Plows)
Settlers saw flat land and thought: farmable.
The Adena had seen the same land and thought: sacred.

When Euro-American settlers moved into the Ohio River Valley in the early 1800s, the Wolfe Plains area began to change fast. The wide, flat plains that made these lands ideal for Adena ceremonial activity also made them perfect for agriculture.

Many of the smaller mounds, the enclosures, the satellite earthworks, the ceremonial rings, didn't survive the 19th century. They were plowed under, quietly, without anyone really noticing what was being lost.
Hartman Mound survived. Partly because it was big, 40 feet tall is hard to plow through. Partly luck. The Wolfe Plains Group originally included 13 identified mound and earthwork sites. Today, most are no longer visible above ground.
plains.jpeg
caption: Illustrated Encyclopedia of Native American Indian Mounds & Earthworks (2016).
credit:
Source: Illustrated Encyclopedia of Native American Indian Mounds & Earthworks (2016)
Illustrated Encyclopedia of Native American Indian Mounds & Earthworks (2016). Mid-1800s survey of the Wolfe Plains complex in Athens, Ohio. There were 10 circular earthworks and 12 large mounds there. It is primarily an Adena & later Hopewell site.

content: The irony of flatness: The same open plains that made Wolfe Plains a perfect ceremonial gathering spot — visible, accessible, central — made it a prime target for agricultural clearing. What worked for the Adena worked for the farmers too, just in opposite directions.
ERA 3 — Early 1900s
William C. Mills Shows Up With a Notebook

Enter William Corless Mills, archaeologist and curator at the Ohio State Archaeological and Historical Society, and arguably the reason we know as much as we do about Wolfe Plains.

In the early 1900s, Mills conducted systematic surveys and excavations of Adena earthwork sites across Ohio. He documented mound structures, mapped site layouts, and recorded burial contents through field notes and excavation reports. His work on Wolfe Plains is one of the earliest formal records of the site.
The artifacts he and other early archaeologists uncovered — pottery, copper ornaments, carved stone pipes, ornamental objects — opened a window into Adena trade networks and artistic traditions that would otherwise be invisible to us today.
map-demo
credit:
Interactive Adena Mound Culture X-Ray.
Source: southeastohiohistory.org. | By Kayla Payne.

content: But there is a complicated side to this story. Excavation methods in the early 1900s were not exactly gentle. Mound interiors were disturbed, burial contexts were lost, and valuable information disappeared in the process. By modern standards, some of what was done to learn about the past also erased parts of it.

The Adena Pipe: Among the most iconic Adena artifacts ever found is the Adena Pipe; a carved effigy pipe depicting a human figure dressed only in a small loincloth. It was likely used in ritual tobacco ceremonies. It's haunting and beautiful and somehow also very, very cool.

Stone Tablets: Carved stone tablets weren't decorative. They were stamping tools used to press patterns onto textiles, animal skins, and possibly human skin during ritual ceremonies.
ERA 4 — Mid-to-Late 1900s
Roads, Suburbs, and Mound Loss
Do you ever look at a place and think, 'There has to be a story here'? Welcome to that feeling.

The mid-20th century brought roads, suburban expansion, and the full transformation of The Plains into the community it is today. US Route 33 carved through the region. Residential streets were laid. The Wolfe Plains Group which had already lost most of its outer earthworks to 19th-century farming now had to contend with active construction.

Several mound sites were built over. Others were left technically intact but practically invisible, buried under lawns, sitting in private backyards, stranded between roads. Fragmented and disconnected from their original spatial relationships with each other.

The Four Main Causes of Loss:

1. Agriculture: Earliest and most widespread damage, but leveled many earthworks.
2. Development: The biggest modern threat, structures built directly over sites.
3. Roads: Fragmented the site, severing mound-to-mound relationships.
4. Erosion: Slow, ongoing and gradually reshaping what remains.

The Folklore Rabbit Hole: Throughout the 19th and 20th centuries, folklore and conspiracy theories circulated claiming the mounds held the remains of giants. Newspapers ran wild with it. The idea was used, at various points, to deny Indigenous people’s credit for building their own monuments (shocking, we know). Archaeological evidence has never supported it. The Adena were fully, ordinarily, impressively human.
ERA 5 — Today
They're Still Here and So Are We.
Against all odds, the mound is still here. Turns out 2,800 years of stubbornness is hard to argue with.

Most of the Wolfe Plains Group's original 13 sites are no longer visible above ground, but some survived. Hartman Mound, standing 40 feet tall sitting in The Plains is the most visible reminder that this neighborhood has a story that goes back way before any of the houses or streets around it. Today, the site is documented, studied, and increasingly protected, thanks to local advocates, archaeologists, and organizations like Friends of the Plains Mound. What we've lost matters. What we still have matters more.
Very few mounds remain fully intact above ground.
Why Should You Care?
This Isn't Just History. It's Your Backyard.

Three Reasons to Care:

1. It's Still There: The Hartman Mound is 40 feet tall and fully visible in The Plains, Ohio. You can go see it today for free.

2. It Connects to a Bigger Story: The Adena people weren't just local, their trade networks stretched across half the continent. They possessed shells from the Gulf and copper from the Great Lakes. Overall, their presence here was part of something enormous.

3. People are working to protect it: The Friends of the Plains Mounds in Athens County, Ohio is a non-profit dedicated to promoting the awareness and protection of the ancient Adena mounds, earthworks, and Indigenous heritage of The Plains. They need people like you to know it exists.
Learn more

Follow Friends of the Plains Mound on Facebook for updates, events, and photographs of the mound. You can also reach them at friendsoftheplains@gmail.com or by mail at P.O. Box 723, Athens, OH 45701.

The mound was here before this town. It'll probably be here after all of us. Maybe wave next time you drive by.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=JegXnhDo2oM

Speaker Alex Armstrong, President of the Friends of the Plains Mounds, explains the Native American burial mounds in The Plains, Ohio, and the surrounding areas. Armstrong provides insight into the ceremonial life and social structure of the early Indigenous peoples of the Athens County area through his presentation.

Sources: Ohio Archaeology (Bradley T. Lepper), Athens County Historical Society Archives, Archaeological Survey Reports of Ohio Earthworks, OhioArchaeology.org, openvirtualworlds.org, ohioslargestplayground.com
